Chunk # 0.
THE JOURNAL OF EXPERIMENTAL ZOOLOGY 230:165-174 (1984) 
Mitochondria From the Hepatopancreas of the Marine Clam 
Mercenaria mercenaria: Substrate Preferences and Salt and pH 
Effects on the Oxidation of Palmitoyl-L-Carnitine and Succinate 
J.S BALLANTYNE AND K.B STOREY 
Institute of Biochemistry, Carleton University, Ottawa, Canada KlS 5B6 
ABSTRACT A method is presented for the isolation of mitochondria with 
good respiratory control from the hepatopancreas of the marine clam Mercen- 
aria mercenaria Palmitoyl-L-carnitine is the preferred substrate of the mito- 
chondria of the hepatopancreas based on state 3 rates of oxidation (in the 
presence of ADP) Rates of oxidation of pyruvate and glutamate were about 
one-half that of the lipid substrate in state 3 a-Glycerophosphate was oxidized 
at a rate about one-third that of palmitoyl-L-carnitine All Krebs cycle inter- 
mediates were oxidized to some extent Proline was not oxidized at detectable 
levels The optimal range of KC1 concentrations for the oxidation of palmitoyl- 
L-carnitine is between 250 and 500 mM whereas the optimal range of KC1 
concentration for the oxidation of succinate is between 200 and 350 mM The 
optimal range of pH for the oxidation of succinate and for the oxidation of 
palmitoyl-L-carnitine lies between pH 6.5 and 7.5 based on the respiratory 
control ratio 
Lipid is the major caloric reserve of the 
molluscan hepatopancreas In the pelagic 
squid, Neptunea, up to 550 moles per gram 
wet weight of the hepatopancreas is lipid 
(Voogt, '72) and in Sepia it represents 20% of 
the dry weight of the hepatopancreas (Bou- 
caud-Camou, '71) Of the total lipid a large 
percentage (64% in Neptunea) is neutral lipid 
Cvoogt, '72) Other metabolic reserves are 
present in the hepatopancreas including car- 
bohydrate in the form of galactogen and gly- 
cogen (van Weel, "74) and amino acids 
(Florkin and Bricteux-Gregoire, '72) It is not 
known which of these energy reserves supply 
the energy requirements of the hepatopan- 
creas itself 
A measure of the importance of substrates 
in supplying the energy needs of a tissue 
may be obtained from studies of the rates of 
oxidation of those substrates by isolated cou- 
pled mitochondria Previous attempts to iso- 
late coupled mitochondria from the mol- 
luscan hepatopancreas (Rees, '53; Zaba et al., 
'78) have been unsuccessful The present 
study presents a method for the isolation of 
intact mitochondria with high respiratory 
control ratios (RCR) from the hepatopancreas 
of the euryhaline clam Mercenaria mercen- 
aria The substrate preferences and the abil- ity to oxidize Krebs cycle intermediates have 
been determined in these mitochondria 
Euryhaline clams may undergo considera- 
ble changes in intracellular inorganic ion 
concentrations during osmotic stress (Schof- 
feniels and Gilles, '72) Additionally these 
animals are frequently subjected to periods 
of anoxia during which acidic end products, 
such as succinate, may acumulate Salt con- 
centration as well as pH may therefore be 
important variables in the function of these 
mitochondria and their effects on the oxida- 
tion of a lipid substrate (palmitoyl-L-carni- 
tine) and succinate (the major anaerobic end 
product of Mercenaria rnercenaria) (Korycan 
and Storey, '83) have been determined 
MATERIALS AND METHODS 
Chemicals and animals 
All biochemicals were purchased from 
Sigma Chemical Co (St Louis, MO) Qua- 
hogs, Mercenaria mercenaria, were obtained 
from a local supplier and held in aerated 
The present address for J.S Ballantyne is Huntsman Marine 
Laboratory, Brandy Cove, St Andrews, New Brunswick, Can- 
ada EOG 2x0 
Address reprint requests to Dr K.B Storey 
0 1984 ALAN R LISS, INC 166 J.S BALLANTYNE AND K.B STOREY 
recirculated sea water (1040 mOsmolA) at 
15°C for periods up to 3 weeks 
Isolation of mitochondria 
The hepatopancreas of five or six clams 
were removed, blotted dry, and placed in 10 
ml of ice-cold isolation medium consisting of 
400 mM sucrose, 1 mM EDTA, 2 mM EGTA, 
and 10 mM Hepes (pH 7.4, at ZOOC), and 1% 
bovine serum albumin (essentially fatty acid 
free) The hepatopancreas were homogenized 
by three or four passes of a Potter-Elvehjem 
homogenizer with a loosely fitting teflon pes- 
tle The homogenate was centrifuged at 200 
g for 10 min and the supernatant was col- 
lected and recentrifuged at 7500 g for 10 min 
The mitochondrial pellet was then resus- 
pended in 10 ml of isolation medium, recen- 
trifuged at 7500 g for 10 min, and the final 
pellet resuspended in a small volume of iso- 
lation medium at a mitochondrial protein 
content of 0.1-0.5 mg/ml 
Oxidative phosphorylation 
Oxygen uptake by mitochondria was mea- 
sured in a 0.71 ml cell (using a Clark type 
oxygen electrode) with temperature main- 
tained at 15°C Assay medium consisted of 
10 mM Hepes (pH 7.2 adjusted at 15°C except 
where pH was varied), 8.6 mM KHzP04, 1% 
BSA, 57 mM sucrose, 0.14 mM EDTA, 0.29 
mM EGTA, and 257 mM KC1 except where 
KC1 concentration was varied

mitochondria.protocol:
**Extracted Steps:**

1. The hepatopancreas of five or six clams were removed, blotted dry, and placed in 10 ml of ice-cold isolation medium consisting of 400 mM sucrose, 1 mM EDTA, 2 mM EGTA, and 10 mM Hepes (pH 7.4, at 20°C), and 1% bovine serum albumin (essentially fatty acid free).
2. The hepatopancreas was homogenized by three or four passes of a Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer with a loosely fitting teflon pestle.
3. The homogenate was centrifuged at 200 g for 10 min and the supernatant was collected.
4. The supernatant was recentrifuged at 7500 g for 10 min.
5. The mitochondrial pellet was then resuspended in 10 ml of isolation medium.
6. The resuspended pellet was recentrifuged at 7500 g for 10 min.
7. The final pellet was resuspended in a small volume of isolation medium at a mitochondrial protein content of 0.1-0.5 mg/ml.

**Incompleteness/uncertainties:**
- The article does not provide information on the specific speed or temperature of the centrifugation steps.
- The article does not mention the duration of the centrifugation steps.
- The article does not specify the type of centrifuge used.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed protocol for the isolation of mitochondria from the hepatopancreas of Mercenaria mercenaria. The steps provided are clear and specific, but there are some gaps in the protocol regarding centrifugation speed, temperature, and duration. These missing details may affect the reproducibility of the protocol.

- Palmitoyl-L-carnitine is the preferred substrate for the mitochondria.
- The oxidation rates of pyruvate and glutamate are about half that of palmitoyl-L-carnitine.
- a-Glycerophosphate is oxidized at a rate about one-third that of palmitoyl-L-carnitine.
- All Krebs cycle intermediates are oxidized to some extent.
- Proline is not oxidized at detectable levels.
- The optimal range of KCl concentrations for the oxidation of palmitoyl-L-carnitine is between 250 and 500 mM, while the optimal range for the oxidation of succinate is between 200 and 350 mM.
- The optimal pH range for the oxidation of both substrates is pH 6.5-7.5.
- Lipid is the major caloric reserve of the molluscan hepatopancreas.
- Other metabolic reserves, including carbohydrate and amino acids, are present in the hepatopancreas.

- Oxygen uptake by the mitochondria was measured using a Clark type oxygen electrode.
- The assay medium consisted of Hepes, KHzP04, BSA, sucrose, EDTA, EGTA, and KC1.
- The study provides insights into the substrate preferences and ability to oxidize Krebs cycle intermediates in the mitochondria.
- The findings have implications for understanding the energy needs of the hepatopancreas and the effects of salt and pH on mitochondrial function.

- Various studies have contributed to understanding respiratory control and metabolic reserves.

- J.S Ballantyne and K.B Storey are researchers affiliated with the Institute of Biochemistry at Carleton University in Ottawa.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The pH of the assay medium was monitored before and after the assay and did not change during the course of the experiments.
2. ADP/O and respiratory control ratios (RCR) were calculated as outlined by Estabrook ('67).
3. Respiration states 3 and 4 were determined as defined by Chance and Williams ('56).
4. All major substrates, including palmitoyl-L-carnitine, were added at concentrations determined to be saturating. The concentration of palmitoyl-L-carnitine did not drop below saturation during the course of the assay.
5. Protein was measured using a modified Biuret procedure with 2% deoxycholete (pH 13) added to solubilize the mitochondria (Gornall et al., '49). Bovine serum albumin was used as the standard.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides steps for monitoring pH, calculating ADP/O and RCR, determining respiration states, adding substrates at saturating concentrations, and measuring protein using a modified Biuret procedure. However, the article does not provide complete mitochondrial isolation steps.

summary: Summary 3: Experiments on ADP/O and respiratory control ratios (RCR) were conducted to study mitochondrial respiration. Mitochondria from HEPATOPANCREAS MITOCHONDRIA of Mercenaria mercenaria were used, and the pH remained constant throughout the experiments. The modified Biuret procedure with deoxycholete was employed to determine protein concentration. Various substrates, including pyruvate, glutamate, succinate, and isocitrate, were tested, and their rates of oxidation and respiratory parameters were measured. Calcium did not enhance oxidation rates, while proline plus pyruvate did not show measurable oxidation. Glutamate and palmitoyl-L-carnitine plus malate had the highest RCRs. Citrate had the highest rate in state 3, while isocitrate and fumarate had lower rates. Succinate and oxaloacetate had rates about half of citrate. State 4 rates were lower for a-ketoglutarate, isocitrate, and fumarate. Citrate and succinate plus rotenone had low RCRs. The effects of KC1 on oxidative phosphorylation with palmitoyl-L-carnitine as the substrate were studied. Both the RCR and the state 3 rates were high and relatively constant between 250 and 500 mM KC1. ADP/O ratios were relatively constant over the entire range tested (85-800 mM). The studies were influenced by the work of Estabrook, Chance and Williams, and Gornall et al.

summary:
**Summary 4**:
Mitochondrial oxidation of Krebs cycle intermediates in the hepatopancreas of Mercenaria mercenaria was studied. The oxidation rates of citrate, DL-isocitrate, alpha-ketoglutarate, succinate, fumarate, malate, and oxaloacetate were measured. The effects of pH and KCl concentration on oxidative phosphorylation were investigated. Optimal pH ranges were identified for the respiratory control ratio (RCR) and state 3 rates, while state 4 rates increased gradually over a wide pH range. ADP/O ratios were highest within a specific pH range. Similarly, optimal KCl concentrations were found for RCR and state 3 rates, while state 4 rates were lowest within this range. ADP/O ratios remained relatively constant within a certain range of KCl concentrations. These findings provide insights into the regulation of mitochondrial oxidation and ATP production in the hepatopancreas of Mercenaria mercenaria.

DISCUSSION 
Some measure of the permeability of the 
inner mitochondria1 membrane to substrates 
can be made by monitoring the stimulation 
of respiration of coupled mitochondria by po- 
tential substrates in the presence of ADP In 
intact mitochondria the presence or absence 
of the appropriate transporter will determine 
if a substrate is oxidized The mitochondria 
isolated from the hepatopancreas of M mer- 
cenaria are apparently permeable to all 
Krebs cycle intermediates tested This may 
be expected in a multifunctional tissue where 
the exchange of metabolites between the cy- 
toplasm and the mitochondrion must occur 
for lipid synthesis, redox balance, and during 
periods of anoxia In particular the high rate 
of oxidation of citrate suggests the inner 
membrane can transport this substrate If 
high rates of transport inward reflect a high 
rate of efflux, mitochondrially produced cit- 
rate may therefore be transported to the cy- 
toplasm for lipid synthesis High activities of 
isocitrate dehydrogenase (NADP) and glu- 
cose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase are present 
in the hepatopancreas of Nassarius (Fried 
and Levin, '73) as required for lipid synthesis 
High rates of oxidation of malate and a- 
ketoglutarate may imply the existence of the 
malate-aspartate shuttle for redox balance 
This would help augment the a-glycerophos- 
phate shuttle activity Hepatopancreas mito- 
chondria are capable of oxidizing a-glyc- 
erophosphate, indicating the presence of the 
a-glycerophosphate cycle In insect flight 
muscle mitochondria the oxidation of a-glyc- 
erophosphate is markedly stimulated by the 
addition of Ca2+ Oxidation of a-glycerophos- 
phate was not stimulated by the addition of 
Ca2+ in the mitochondria of the hepatopan- 
creas of M mercenaria This lack of Ca2+ 
stimulation was also observed in the mito- 
chondria isolated from the ventricle of M 
mercenaria CBallantyne and Storey, '83) A 
possible explanation lies in the study of Wer- 
nette et al ('81) which indicates that calcium 
stimulation of a-glycerophosphate dehydro- 
genase in rat liver mitochondria decreases 
the Km for a-glycerophosphate Thus at sat- 
urating levels of a-glycerophosphate, such as 
those used in the present study, no stimula- 
tion would be observed 
Proline is an important substrate in many molluscan tissues including the cephalopod 
heart (Ballantyne et al., '81) and the clam 
heart (Ballantyne and Storey, '83) However, 
the mitochondria of the hepatopancreas of M 
mercenaria do not oxidize proline at detect- 
able levels Zaba et al ('78) found mitochon- 
dria from the hepatopancreas of Mytilus 
edulis also failed to oxidize proline while Mo- 
mmsen et al ('83) reported that COa produc- 
tion from proline by tissue slices of the 
hepatopancreas of the pelagic squid Symplec 
toteuthis was the lowest of all the tisues 
tested The same authors reported low pro- 
line levels (1.93 mM) in the hepatopancreas 
further confirming the relative unimport- 
ance of this substrate in the hepatopancreas, 
in contrast to the heart mitochondria of ma- 
rine molluscs where it is a major substrate 
(Ballantyne et al., '81; Ballantyne and Sto- 
rey, '83) Thus the oxidation of proline by M 
mercenaria mitochondria appears to be tis- 
sue specific; proline is oxidized by the mito- 
chondria of the heart but not by those of the 
hepatopancreas 
While proline is not oxidized by hepatopan- 
creas mitochondria, glutamate is readily ox- 
idized Glutamate is an intermediate in the 
oxidation of several amino acids including 
proline, arginine, and ornithine Glutamate 
oxidation by mitochondria from the hepato- 
pancreas of M mercenaria may suggest that 
other amino acids, such as arginine and or- 
nithine, may be oxidized in the hepatopan- 
creas via glutamate It would appear that the 
enzymes required for the oxidation of proline 
to glutamate are either absent or are under 
strict control in this tissue 
The rates of oxidation of succinate by he- 
patopancreas mitochondria are low when 
compared to those for insect and vertebrate 
tissues where succinate is usually oxidized at 
the highest rates with respect to the other 
substrates (Hansford, '75)

Mitochondria in the hepatopancreas of Mercenaria mereenaria play a crucial role in energy metabolism. The oxidation of succinate by these mitochondria is relatively low compared to other tissues. The RCR and ADP/O ratios are affected by pH levels, while state 3 and state 4 rates of substrate oxidation are influenced by pH as well. The malate-aspartate shuttle and α-glycerophosphate shuttle are involved in redox balance. Ca2+ stimulates the oxidation of α-glycerophosphate. Proline oxidation is tissue-specific, while glutamate is readily oxidized. These findings are consistent with studies by Ballantyne et al., Ballantyne and Storey, Fried and Levin, Hansford, Mommsen et al., Zaba et al ('78), and Wernet et al ('81).

article:
Chunk # 4.
The lower rate of 
oxidation of succinate by the mitochondria of 
the hepatopancreas of the clam may be due 
to the presence of a transporter designed 
more for succinate efflux from the mitochon- 
dria During anoxia mitochondrially gener- 
ated succinate passes into the cytoplasm and 
hence to the extrapallial fluid where it is 
neutralized by complexing with calcium de- 
rived from the shell (Crenshaw and Neff, '69) 
Lipid represents the largest pool of caloric 
reserves in the molluscan hepatopancreas 
Woogt, '72) This is reflected in the substrate 
preferences of the mitochondria isolated from 
the hepatopancreas of M mercenaria Palmi- HEPATOPANCREAS MITOCHONDRIA 173 
toyl-L-carnitine was oxidized at the highest 
rates of any of the substrates tested Lower 
rates of oxidation of pyruvate by these mito- 
chondria and the low glycogen levels re- 
ported for this tissue (Korycan and Storey, 
’83) suggests that carbohydrate plays a lesser 
role in the energy supply of the molluscan 
hepatopancreas Rees (’53) found octanoate 
was not utilized by subcellular preparations 
of hepatopancreas in Helix Carnitine would 
therefore seem essential to the oxidation of 
even short chain fatty acids in this tissue 
The heart mitochondria of squids (Ballan- 
tyne et al., ’81; Mommsen and Hochachka, 
’81) and M mercenaria (Ballantyne and Sto- 
rey, ’83) are all incapable of oxidizing lipid 
as palmitoyl-L-carnitine The cephalopod 
mantle also has very low activities of the 
lipid oxidizing enzyme 0-hydroxyacyl CoA 
dehydrogenase (Mommsen et al., ’81) The 
hepatopancreas therefore appears to be the 
only molluscan tissue yet studied to prefer 
lipid as a substrate As such it must play a 
major role in the intermediary metabolism 
of fats for the whole animal 
Euryhaline molluscs are osmoconformers 
and cell volume regulators over a wide range 
of salinities (Pierce, ’82) Intracellular inor- 
ganic ion concentrations may change by sev- 
eral-fold (Schoffeniels and Gilles, ’72) Lipid 
oxidation by M mercenaria hepatopancreas 
mitochondria shows optimal RCR and state 
3 rates between 250 and 500 mM Kcl The 
oxidation of this substrate may therefore pro- 
ceed with high efficiency and rate in hypoos- 
motic environments ranging from half to full 
strength sea water (500-1000 mOsm) This 
range may be termed the “o~moneutral’~ 
zone At concentrations higher than sea 
water a decreased efficiency and rate of oxi- 
dation of lipid occurs Thus the metabolic 
adaptation to changing salinities (those out- 
side the “osmoneutral” zone) may involve, in 
part, alterations at the mitochondrial level 
that change the optimal salt concentration 
for mitochondrial substrate oxidations 
The range of optimal salt concentration for 
state 3 and RCR for the oxidation of palmi- 
toyl-L-carnitine by mitochondria isolated 
from the hepatopancreas is quantitatively 
similar to that observed for proline oxidation 
in the isolated mitochondria of the ventricle 
of M mercenaria (Ballantyne and Storey, ’83) 
Although this similarity suggests that re- 
gardless of the substrate oxidized, aerobic 
metabolism in each tissue functions over the 
same range of salinity, the oxidation of suc- cinate does not follow this pattern 
The optimal range of salt concentration for

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The hepatopancreas of the clam has a lower rate of oxidation of succinate by mitochondria, possibly due to the presence of a transporter designed for succinate efflux from the mitochondria.
2. During anoxia, mitochondrially generated succinate passes into the cytoplasm and then to the extrapallial fluid, where it is neutralized by complexing with calcium derived from the shell.
3. The hepatopancreas of molluscs has a large pool of lipid reserves, reflected in the substrate preferences of mitochondria isolated from the hepatopancreas.
4. Palmitoyl-L-carnitine is oxidized at the highest rates among the tested substrates.
5. Pyruvate is oxidized at lower rates by these mitochondria, and the low glycogen levels in the tissue suggest that carbohydrates play a lesser role in the energy supply of the hepatopancreas.
6. Octanoate is not utilized by subcellular preparations of the hepatopancreas.
7. Carnitine is essential for the oxidation of even short-chain fatty acids in this tissue.
8. Heart mitochondria of squids and M. mercenaria are incapable of oxidizing lipid as palmitoyl-L-carnitine.
9. The cephalopod mantle also has very low activities of the lipid oxidizing enzyme 0-hydroxyacyl CoA dehydrogenase.
10. The hepatopancreas is the only molluscan tissue studied so far that prefers lipid as a substrate and plays a major role in the intermediary metabolism of fats for the whole animal.
11. Euryhaline molluscs are osmoconformers and cell volume regulators over a wide range of salinities.
12. Intracellular inorganic ion concentrations may change several-fold.
13. Lipid oxidation by M. mercenaria hepatopancreas mitochondria shows optimal rates between 250 and 500 mM KCl.
14. The oxidation of palmityl-L-carnitine may proceed with high efficiency and rate in hypoosmotic environments ranging from half to full strength seawater.
15. The optimal salt concentration for the oxidation of succinate is lower (200-350 mM) than that of palmitoyl-L-carnitine.
16. Succinate accumulates to high levels (15-25 mM) in many tissues of M. mercenaria during anoxia.
17. The hepatopancreas is a likely candidate for the recycling of accumulated succinate during recovery from anoxia.
18. The effects of pH and salinity on the oxidation of succinate may be crucial.
19. The pH range for efficient oxidation of succinate is the same as that observed with palmitoyl-L-carnitine as a substrate (pH 6.5-7.5).
20. The behavior of the succinate state 4 rate differs from that of palmitoyl-L-carnitine and proline in this tissue.
21. State 4 rates of succinate oxidation increase continuously over the tested range, but the reason for this phenomenon is unknown.
22. High respiratory control ratio (RCR) for lipid oxidation is observed between pH 6.5 and 7.2, while optimal rates in state 3 occur over a narrower range (pH 7.0-7.2).
23. Efficient oxidation of lipid may occur during recovery from anoxia (low pH), but at reduced rates.
24. The study only investigates the separate effects of salt and pH on mitochondrial metabolism in M. mercenaria.
25. The range of salt tolerance (osmoneutral zone) where RCR is high and acclimation would not appear necessary is wider for NAD-linked substrates compared to flavin-linked substrates.

summary: Revised Summary: Mitochondria in the hepatopancreas of clams and squids play a crucial role in the oxidation of succinate and lipid metabolism. Succinate, a key molecule in intermediary metabolism, accumulates during anoxia and is efficiently recycled during recovery. The oxidation of succinate is influenced by pH and salinity, with a lower salt tolerance compared to lipid oxidation. These mitochondria are important for osmoconformers and cell volume regulation, maintaining optimal intracellular ion concentrations. Transporters and lipid oxidizing enzymes are essential for efficient metabolism. Studies by Crenshaw, Neff, Korycan, Storey, and Ballantyne have explored the substrate preferences and oxidation efficiency of succinate in different molluscan species.

summary: Revised Summary: This study investigates the combined stress of lower pH and varying salinity on the optimal oxidation range of substrates. Supported by a grant from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada and an NSERC postdoctoral fellowship, the research was aided by Prof S Jacobson from Carleton University's Department of Biology and involved discussions with Dr R Hansford. The study explores the migration of squid, focusing on enzymes, tissues, and mitochondrial respiratory control. It examines the hepatopancreas in organisms like Mercenaria mercenaria and Nassarius obsoletus, and determines protein levels using the biuret reaction. The respiratory and enzymatic properties of squid heart mitochondria are analyzed, along with lipid constituents in the liver of Sepia officinalis. The research also investigates substrate preferences and praline oxidation, decalcification in marine clams, mitochondrial respiratory control, polarographic measurement, nitrogen metabolism in mollusks, and organ-specific metabolism. In addition, invertebrate cell volume mechanisms, osmoregulation, ionoregulation, lipid and sterol components, and the glycerophosphate dehydrogenase enzyme in rat liver mitochondria are studied. Mitochondrial properties are examined in the sea mussel Mytilus edulis. The study draws on various literature and studies to enhance our understanding of these topics.
